# Contemporary Theories of Consciousness {#contemporary-theories}

## Chapter Overview

This chapter surveys the leading contemporary scientific theories of consciousness, focusing on their mechanisms, predictions, and implications for the relationship between life and mind.

---

## 10.1 Global Workspace Theory (GWT)

- Baars: consciousness as a global broadcast in the brain
- The "theatre" metaphor: spotlight of attention on a global stage
- Neural Global Workspace (Dehaene and Changeux):
  - Prefrontal-parietal network
  - Ignition and sustained activity
- Empirical evidence: masking, attentional blink, binocular rivalry
- Predictions and testability

## 10.2 Integrated Information Theory (IIT)

- Tononi: consciousness as integrated information (Φ)
- The five axioms and five postulates
- Consciousness as intrinsic causal power
- IIT as a mathematical framework
- Implications:
  - Consciousness is graded, not binary
  - Even simple systems may have minimal Φ
  - Panpsychist implications
- Criticisms: unfalsifiability, counterintuitive predictions

## 10.3 Predictive Processing and the Free Energy Principle

- The brain as a prediction machine (Clark, Hohwy)
- Friston's Free Energy Principle:
  - All living systems minimize surprise (free energy)
  - Perception, action, and learning as inference
- Active inference and consciousness
- Implications for the central question:
  - If all living systems minimize free energy, do all living systems have experience?
  - Consciousness as a byproduct of prediction?

## 10.4 Attention Schema Theory (AST)

- Graziano: consciousness as the brain's model of its own attention
- The attention schema as a simplified, useful fiction
- Why the brain "believes" it is conscious
- Implications for AI: build an attention schema, get (functional) consciousness?

## 10.5 Recurrent Processing Theory

- Lamme: consciousness arises from recurrent (feedback) neural processing
- Feedforward processing is unconscious
- Recurrent processing creates phenomenal experience
- Empirical evidence from visual neuroscience
- Implications: a clear neural threshold for consciousness

## 10.6 Orchestrated Objective Reduction (Orch-OR) — Brief Preview

- Penrose and Hameroff: quantum processes in microtubules
- Why it is listed here but treated fully in Chapter 13
- Its relationship to other theories

## 10.7 Theory Comparison

- How the major theories differ on:
  - What consciousness is
  - Where it exists
  - Whether it is graded or binary
  - Whether it extends beyond brains
  - Whether it is testable
- Table: comparative summary of GWT, IIT, FEP, AST, RPT

## 10.8 The Adversarial Collaboration

- The structured empirical tests pitting GWT against IIT
- Results so far and what they tell us
- The limits of empirical adjudication between theories

## 10.9 Implications for the Central Question

- GWT and RPT → consciousness requires specific neural architecture → life first
- IIT → consciousness is graded and potentially universal → co-emergence or consciousness first
- FEP → consciousness linked to all living systems → co-emergence
- AST → consciousness as a model, could exist in any modeling system → substrate-independent

## Chapter Summary

- Key takeaways
- Open question: Will empirical science resolve the debate between theories, or is the disagreement fundamentally philosophical?
